q: Gautama Buddha was born in which place (in modern Nepal)?
options: Lumbini (in the Sakya republic, modern Nepal)
explanation: Gautama Buddha was born in Lumbini (in the Sakya republic, in the foothills of the Himalayas, in modern Nepal) — c. 563 BCE. His father was Suddhodana (king of the Sakya clan) and his mother was Mahamaya (who died shortly after his birth; his stepmother Mahaprajapati raised him). His birthplace Lumbini is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1997). The Buddha's personal name was Siddhartha; his clan name was Gautama (Gotama).

q: Gautama Buddha attained enlightenment (Bodhi) under which tree and at which place?
explanation: Gautama Buddha attained enlightenment (Bodhi) under the Bodhi tree (a peepal/Ficus religiosa tree) at Bodh Gaya (in modern Bihar) — c. 528 BCE, at the age of 35. After 49 days of meditation, he became the 'Buddha' (the Enlightened One). Bodh Gaya is one of the four most important Buddhist pilgrimage sites (the others: Lumbini birth, Sarnath first sermon, Kushinagar death). The Mahabodhi Temple at Bodh Gaya is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (2002). Bihar is the place of the Buddha's enlightenment.

q: Buddha delivered his first sermon (Dharmachakra Pravartana, 'turning the wheel of Dharma') at:
explanation: Buddha delivered his first sermon (Dharmachakra Pravartana, 'turning the wheel of Dharma') at Sarnath (the Deer Park, near Varanasi, UP) — c. 528 BCE, shortly after his enlightenment. He taught the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path to his five former companions (the first five disciples = the first Buddhist Sangha). Sarnath is one of the four most important Buddhist pilgrimage sites. The Dhamek Stupa at Sarnath marks the spot of the first sermon.

q: Buddha attained Mahaparinirvana (death/final liberation) at:
explanation: Buddha attained Mahaparinirvana (death/final liberation) at Kushinagar (in modern UP) — c. 483 BCE, at the age of 80. His last words were: 'All conditioned things are impermanent. Work out your liberation with diligence.' His body was cremated, and his relics were divided among eight kingdoms — and stupas were built over them. The Mahaparinibbana Sutta (the Buddhist text describing the Buddha's last days) records that his last meal was offered by Chunda (a blacksmith) at Pava — and that he fell ill after the meal.

q: The Four Noble Truths (Arya Satya) of Buddhism are:
options: Right view, right intention, right speech, right action
explanation: The Four Noble Truths (Arya Satya) are: (1) Dukkha — life is suffering; (2) Samudaya — the cause of suffering is desire (trishna/craving); (3) Nirodha — suffering can be ended by ending desire; (4) Magga — the path to end suffering (the Eightfold Path). These are the core teachings of Buddhism — taught in the first sermon at Sarnath. The Four Noble Truths are the Buddha's diagnosis of the human condition: suffering exists, it has a cause, it can be ended, and there is a path to end it.

q: The Eightfold Path (Astangika Magga) of Buddhism includes:
options: Right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration
explanation: The Eightfold Path (Astangika Magga): (1) Right view (understanding the Four Noble Truths); (2) Right intention (renunciation, goodwill, harmlessness); (3) Right speech (no lying, slander, harsh speech, gossip); (4) Right action (no killing, stealing, sexual misconduct); (5) Right livelihood (no harmful occupations); (6) Right effort (cultivating good, abandoning evil); (7) Right mindfulness (awareness of body, feelings, mind, phenomena); (8) Right concentration (meditation/samadhi). These 8 are grouped into 3: wisdom (1-2), morality (3-5), meditation (6-8).

q: Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara of Jainism, was born at:
explanation: Mahavira (the 24th and last Tirthankara of Jainism) was born at Kundagrama (a suburb of Vaishali, in modern Bihar) — c. 599 BCE. His father was Siddhartha (the leader of the Jnatrika clan) and his mother was Trishala (a Licchavi princess of Vaishali). His personal name was Vardhamana. Mahavira was a contemporary of the Buddha. He renounced his home at age 30 and attained Kevala Jnana (omniscience) at age 42 after 12 years of severe asceticism. Vaishali (Bihar) is the birthplace of Mahavira — a key Bihar connection for Jainism.

q: Mahavira attained Nirvana (death) at which place (in Bihar)?
explanation: Mahavira attained Nirvana (death/final liberation) at Pawapuri (in modern Bihar, Nalanda district) — c. 527 BCE, at the age of 72. Pawapuri is a major Jain pilgrimage site — the Jal Mandir (a temple in the middle of a lotus pond) marks the spot of his cremation. After Mahavira's death, Jainism split into two sects: (1) Digambara ('sky-clad' — monks wear no clothes; dominant in South India); (2) Svetambara ('white-clad' — monks wear white robes; dominant in West India). Bihar is where Jainism was founded and where Mahavira died.

q: Buddhism and Jainism both emerged in which century BCE?
explanation: Both Buddhism and Jainism emerged in the 6th century BCE (c. 600-500 BCE) — as a reaction against the rigid Vedic ritualism and the caste system. The Buddha (c. 563-483 BCE) and Mahavira (c. 599-527 BCE) were contemporaries — both preached in the Magadha-Kosala region (primarily in modern Bihar and UP). Both religions: (1) rejected the authority of the Vedas; (2) rejected the caste system; (3) emphasised non-violence (ahimsa); (4) used the vernacular languages (Pali for Buddhism, Ardhamagadhi for Jainism) instead of Sanskrit; (5) attracted merchants and artisans (not just Brahmins and Kshatriyas).

q: The Tripitaka ('Three Baskets') is the sacred text of which religion?
explanation: The Tripitaka ('Three Baskets') is the sacred text of Buddhism. The three baskets are: (1) Sutta Pitaka (the Buddha's discourses — the teachings); (2) Vinaya Pitaka (the monastic rules — the discipline); (3) Abhidhamma Pitaka (the philosophical analysis — the doctrine). The Tripitaka was compiled at the First Buddhist Council (Rajgir, 483 BCE) and finalized at the Third Council (Pataliputra, 250 BCE). The Tripitaka is written in Pali (the language of the Theravada tradition). The Mahayana tradition uses Sanskrit texts (the Mahayana Sutras).

q: The sacred texts of Jainism are called:
explanation: The sacred texts of Jainism are called Agamas (the 'coming' — the teachings that 'come' from the Tirthankaras). The Agamas are based on the teachings of Mahavira (the 24th Tirthankara) — compiled by his disciples (the Ganadharas). The Agamas are written in Ardhamagadhi Prakrit (the language of the common people of Magadha). There are 12 Angas (the main texts) + 12 Upangas (subsidiary) + other texts. The Svetambaras accept 45 Agamas; the Digambaras accept a different set (they believe the original texts were lost).

q: The principle of 'Ahimsa' (non-violence) is most strictly followed in which religion?
explanation: Jainism follows the strictest form of Ahimsa (non-violence) — Jains avoid harming any living being (including insects, plants, and microorganisms). Jain monks wear masks (mukhavastrika) to avoid inhaling insects; sweep the ground before walking (to avoid stepping on insects); filter water (to avoid swallowing microorganisms); and avoid root vegetables (because uprooting a plant kills it and the microorganisms on it). Buddhism also teaches ahimsa, but less strictly — the Buddha allowed eating meat (if the animal was not killed specifically for the monk). Hinduism also values ahimsa, but it is most strict in Jainism.

q: How many Tirthankaras are there in Jainism?
explanation: There are 24 Tirthankaras in Jainism — the spiritual teachers who 'ford the river' (tirtha) of samsara (the cycle of rebirth). The first Tirthankara was Rishabhanatha (Adinatha) — and the 24th and last was Mahavira (c. 599-527 BCE). The 23rd Tirthankara was Parshvanatha (c. 8th century BCE — the first historically attested Tirthankara before Mahavira). The Tirthankaras are not founders of Jainism — Jainism is considered eternal (sanatana), and the Tirthankaras rediscover and teach it in each cosmic cycle.

q: The Buddhist Sangha (monastic community) consisted of:
explanation: The Buddhist Sangha (monastic community) consisted of four groups: (1) Bhikkhus (monks); (2) Bhikkhunis (nuns — the Buddha allowed women to join, though reluctantly, at the request of his stepmother Mahaprajapati and his disciple Ananda); (3) Upasakas (laymen — who follow the Five Precepts); (4) Upasikas (laywomen). The Sangha was open to all — regardless of caste, gender, or social status. This was revolutionary in the caste-ridden Vedic society. The Sangha was governed by the Vinaya Pitaka (the monastic rules).

q: The Five Precepts (Pancha Sila) of Buddhism are:
options: No killing, no stealing, no sexual misconduct, no lying, no intoxication
explanation: The Five Precepts (Pancha Sila) are the basic ethical rules for Buddhist lay followers: (1) No killing (ahimsa); (2) No stealing; (3) No sexual misconduct; (4) No lying (truthfulness); (5) No intoxication (no alcohol/drugs — because intoxication leads to loss of mindfulness). Monks and nuns follow additional rules (the Vinaya — 227 rules for monks, 311 for nuns in the Theravada tradition). The Five Precepts are the minimum ethical standard for all Buddhists.

q: Buddhism split into two main branches at the Fourth Buddhist Council (c. 72 CE). These are:
explanation: Buddhism split into two main branches at the Fourth Buddhist Council (Kashmir, c. 72 CE, under Kanishka): (1) Hinayana ('Lesser Vehicle' — later called Theravada, 'Way of the Elders') — conservative; emphasises individual liberation (Arhat); uses Pali; follows the Tripitaka; dominant in Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos. (2) Mahayana ('Greater Vehicle') — liberal; emphasises universal liberation (Bodhisattva — one who seeks enlightenment for all beings); uses Sanskrit; developed new texts (the Mahayana Sutras); dominant in China, Japan, Korea, Tibet, Vietnam. A third branch — Vajrayana ('Diamond Vehicle' — Tantric Buddhism) — later emerged in Tibet.

q: Jainism split into two main sects after Mahavira's death. These are:
explanation: Jainism split into two sects after Mahavira's death (c. 527 BCE) — the split formalised c. 79 CE: (1) Digambara ('sky-clad' — monks wear no clothes; believe in complete renunciation; dominant in South India — Karnataka, Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu). (2) Svetambara ('white-clad' — monks wear white robes; believe that complete nudity is not necessary; dominant in West India — Gujarat, Rajasthan). Key differences: Digambaras believe that women cannot achieve moksha (they must be reborn as men first); Svetambaras believe women can achieve moksha. Digambaras accept fewer Agamas; Svetambaras accept 45 Agamas.

q: The three jewels (Triratna) of Buddhism are:
options: Four Noble Truths, Eightfold Path, Five Precepts
explanation: The Three Jewels (Triratna / Triple Gem) of Buddhism: (1) Buddha (the teacher — the Enlightened One); (2) Dharma (the teaching — the Four Noble Truths and Eightfold Path); (3) Sangha (the community — the monastic order). Every Buddhist takes refuge in the Three Jewels: 'I take refuge in the Buddha, I take refuge in the Dharma, I take refuge in the Sangha.' This is the core declaration of Buddhist faith — equivalent to the Islamic Shahada or the Christian baptism.

q: The three jewels (Triratna) of Jainism are:
options: Right faith (Samyak Darshana), Right knowledge (Samyak Jnana), Right conduct (Samyak Charitra)
explanation: The Three Jewels (Triratna) of Jainism: (1) Samyak Darshana (Right faith — belief in the Tirthankaras and the Jain teachings); (2) Samyak Jnana (Right knowledge — understanding the true nature of reality: that the soul (jiva) and matter (ajiva) are distinct); (3) Samyak Charitra (Right conduct — following the Five Vows: ahimsa, satya, asteya, brahmacharya, aparigraha). These three are the path to liberation (moksha) in Jainism — one must have all three together (faith + knowledge + conduct).

q: The Five Vows (Pancha Mahavrata) of Jainism are:
explanation: The Five Vows (Pancha Mahavrata) of Jainism: (1) Ahimsa (non-violence — the strictest form); (2) Satya (truthfulness); (3) Asteya (non-stealing — not taking what is not given); (4) Brahmacharya (celibacy/chastity); (5) Aparigraha (non-possession — detachment from material things). These five vows are stricter for monks and nuns (Mahavrata — 'great vows') and less strict for lay followers (Anuvrata — 'small vows'). Aparigraha (non-possession) is uniquely Jain — it means that attachment to possessions is a cause of karma and suffering.

q: Bihar's connection with Buddhism includes which of the following sites?
options: All of the above (Bodh Gaya enlightenment, Rajgir first council, Vaishali second council, Nalanda university, Pataliputra third council)
explanation: Bihar is the cradle of Buddhism — all the most important Buddhist events happened in Bihar: (1) Bodh Gaya — the Buddha attained enlightenment (c. 528 BCE); (2) Rajgir — the First Buddhist Council (c. 483 BCE) + the Buddha's monastery (Veluvana); (3) Vaishali — the Second Buddhist Council (c. 383 BCE) + the Buddha's last sermon; (4) Pataliputra — the Third Buddhist Council (c. 250 BCE, Ashoka); (5) Nalanda — the great Buddhist university (5th-12th century CE). Bihar is the most important Buddhist pilgrimage destination in the world — especially Bodh Gaya (where the Mahabodhi Temple is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, 2002).

q: Bihar's connection with Jainism includes which of the following sites?
options: All of the above (Vaishali birthplace of Mahavira, Pawapuri death of Mahavira, Rajgir first Jain council)
explanation: Bihar is also the cradle of Jainism — the key events of Mahavira's life happened in Bihar: (1) Vaishali (Kundagrama) — the birthplace of Mahavira (c. 599 BCE); (2) Pawapuri (Nalanda district) — Mahavira's Nirvana/death (c. 527 BCE); (3) Rajgir — the first Jain council (c. 527 BCE, at Pataliputra — some sources say it was at Pataliputra, under Sthulabhadra). Bihar has many important Jain pilgrimage sites — especially Pawapuri (the Jal Mandir), Vaishali, and Rajgir. The state has a significant Jain heritage — though Jainism is now more prominent in Gujarat and Rajasthan.

q: Nalanda University, the great Buddhist centre of learning, was located in:
explanation: Nalanda University was located in Bihar (near Rajgir, in modern Nalanda district) — one of the world's first residential universities (5th-12th century CE). It was founded by the Gupta king Kumargupta I (c. 427 CE) and patronised by later Gupta, Pala, and Harsha rulers. At its peak, Nalanda had 10,000 students, 2,000 teachers, and a library (Dharmaganja) with millions of manuscripts. Subjects: Buddhism, logic, grammar, medicine, astronomy, mathematics. Students came from Tibet, China, Korea, Japan, and Central Asia. Destroyed by Bakhtiyar Khalji (c. 1200 CE). UNESCO World Heritage Site (2016).

q: Which Buddhist council was held at Pataliputra (Bihar) under Ashoka?
explanation: The Third Buddhist Council was held at Pataliputra (Bihar, c. 250 BCE) — under the patronage of Emperor Ashoka (Maurya). It was presided over by Moggaliputta Tissa. The council: (1) compiled the Abhidhamma Pitaka (the third basket of the Tripitaka — the philosophical analysis); (2) purged the Sangha of heretical monks; (3) sent Buddhist missionaries to various countries (Sri Lanka, Myanmar, Greece, Central Asia) — beginning the spread of Buddhism beyond India. The Third Council was crucial in transforming Buddhism from an Indian sect to a world religion.

q: The Buddha and Mahavira were contemporaries. Which statement about them is correct?
options: They never lived in the same region
Both preached in the Magadha-Kosala region (primarily in modern Bihar and UP)
explanation: The Buddha (c. 563-483 BCE) and Mahavira (c. 599-527 BCE) were contemporaries — both preached in the Magadha-Kosala region (primarily in modern Bihar and UP). They likely never met (though Buddhist texts mention Mahavira as 'Nigantha Nataputta'). Both religions: (1) rejected the Vedas and caste system; (2) emphasised ahimsa; (3) used vernacular languages (Pali for Buddhism, Ardhamagadhi for Jainism); (4) attracted merchants and artisans; (5) were patronised by Magadhan kings (Bimbisara and Ajatashatru for Buddhism; Bimbisara also supported Jainism). The rise of Buddhism and Jainism in the 6th century BCE is called the 'Sramana movement' — a reaction against Vedic Brahmanism.
